data.title: How Much Money Do You Need to Live Off Dividends Forever? Here's The Portfolio That Replaces An Upper-Class Income
data.description:
The fantasy usually starts the same way: no alarm clock, no boss, no Sunday-night dread spiral. Just a brokerage account quietly dropping cash into the account while life carries on.

Then the calculator shows up.

For households hoping to generate roughly $175,000 to $200,000 annually from dividends alone, the math points to a portfolio somewhere around $5 million to $7 million depending on yield, taxes, and risk tolerance.

Don't Miss:

What Counts As Upper-Class Income Today?

Upper-income status generally starts at more than double the national median household income.

Recent Pew Research analysis and income data place the national median household income around $83,000, while upper-income households typically earn roughly $170,000 to $180,000 or more annually.

Of course, geography changes everything. A $175,000 income stretches very differently in rural America than it does in places where parking spots somehow cost the same as starter homes did a generation ago.

For this article, the target range is roughly $175,000 to $200,000 in annual pre-tax dividend income. That level can support a comfortable lifestyle with travel, healthcare, retirement savings, dining out, and flexibility without relying on earned wages.

Why Dividend Investors Need Bigger Portfolios

The formula behind dividend investing is straightforward:

Desired Annual Income ÷ Dividend Yield = Portfolio Size Needed

The challenge is yield.

The broader S&P 500 currently yields roughly 1%, which means investors trying to generate $175,000 annually from index-level dividends alone would need a portfolio well north of $15 million.

Trending: Real estate, crypto, private deals, and more—see why investors are turning to self-directed IRAs through IRA Financial

That is why many dividend-focused investors build portfolios yielding closer to 2.5% to 3.5% using dividend-paying stocks, dividend-focused ETFs, utilities, REITs, healthcare companies, consumer staples, and other income-producing assets.

At a 3% yield, the math looks more manageable:

  • $150,000 annual income requires about $5 million
  • $175,000 annual income requires about $5.83 million
  • $200,000 annual income requires about $6.67 million
  • $250,000 annual income requires about $8.33 million

Many retirees instead follow the traditional 4% rule, withdrawing roughly 4% annually from a diversified portfolio while occasionally selling shares.

Under that framework, generating $175,000 annually would require closer to $4.4 million instead of nearly $6 million through dividends alone.

Dividend-focused investors take a different approach. Rather than selling investments over time, they aim to live entirely off portfolio income while preserving the underlying assets. The tradeoff is needing significantly more capital upfront in exchange for the psychological comfort of leaving the core portfolio untouched.

Of course, higher yields are not always better. Sometimes a stock paying 9% is a hidden gem. Other times it is Wall Street's version of a smoke alarm.

That is why many long-term income investors focus on companies with durable balance sheets, manageable payout ratios, and long histories of increasing dividends instead of simply chasing the biggest yield available.

Taxes Can Quietly Change The Equation

A portfolio producing six figures in dividend income may look incredible before taxes. After taxes, the picture changes fast.

Qualified dividends generally receive favorable federal tax treatment, but higher earners can still face effective federal rates ranging from roughly 15% to 23.8% depending on income levels and the net investment income tax.

State taxes can take another bite depending on location.

That means investors may ultimately need portfolios 15% to 25% larger than expected to comfortably net their desired spending amount after taxes.

Inflation also matters. A portfolio producing $175,000 today may not deliver the same purchasing power 15 years from now unless dividend payments continue growing over time. That is one reason many dividend investors gravitate toward companies with long records of annual payout increases rather than focusing only on current yield.

Living off dividends forever is possible.

It just turns out the road to never needing a paycheck again usually requires building a portfolio large enough to make most people suddenly very interested in going back to work one more year.

data.title: Kevin O'Leary Says Banning Wall Street From Housing Is 'Government Trying To Manipulate Markets' — And Warns 'History Shows That Usually Ends Badly'
data.description:
For years, Wall Street has been cast as the villain of the housing market. Now "Shark Tank" Kevin O'Leary is arguing something far more uncomfortable: without institutional money, the market might have looked even uglier.

O'Leary entered the housing debate defending institutional investors at a time when lawmakers are pushing to crack down on corporate ownership of single-family homes.

"Institutional investors are being blamed for everything happening in housing right now," O'Leary wrote on X last week. "But people forget what happened during the financial crisis."

He then pointed to the 2008 housing collapse, arguing institutional buyers were among the few major sources of capital still purchasing real estate while markets were unraveling.

"Without it, things could have been far worse for average Americans," O'Leary wrote. 

"That's why I don't like policies designed to push institutions out of housing," he continued. "It's government trying to manipulate markets, and history shows that usually ends badly."

Don't Miss:

The Housing Market's Favorite Villain Is Back In The Spotlight

O'Leary's comments arrive as frustration over housing affordability continues boiling over across the country.

Lawmakers from both parties have introduced proposals aimed at limiting large investors from buying additional single-family homes. Some measures would restrict purchases outright, while others would impose taxes or ownership caps on firms holding hundreds of properties.

Critics argue institutional buyers crowd out ordinary families and turn neighborhoods into giant rental portfolios. Supporters counter that the issue is far more complicated, pointing instead to years of underbuilding, zoning restrictions, and inventory shortages.

O'Leary clearly falls into the second camp.

"The flow of pension funds, sovereign wealth, and institutional capital is a foundational part of the economy," he wrote.

His broader point was not that housing affordability problems are fake. It was that politicians blaming large investors for everything may be oversimplifying a housing market with problems decades in the making.

Trending: The Midwest multifamily trend attracting institutional capital and accredited investors alike

Wall Street Owns Fewer Homes Than Many Think

Nationally, large institutional investors still own a relatively small slice of the single-family housing market. Estimates generally place their share around 0.5% to 1% of all U.S. single-family homes.

That figure rises sharply in certain Sun Belt markets where institutional ownership became more concentrated after the Great Recession. Cities like Atlanta, Jacksonville, Florida and Charlotte, North Carolina have seen significantly higher levels of investor-owned rental homes.

That local impact helps explain why emotions around the issue run so hot.

To many renters and younger buyers staring at impossible monthly payments, Wall Street-backed firms purchasing homes can feel less like "market liquidity" and more like trying to outbid a hedge fund armed with unlimited cash.

Still, O'Leary's warning about government intervention reflects a broader investing philosophy he has repeated for years: markets usually react unpredictably when policymakers attempt to force outcomes.

And history does provide examples where abrupt interventions created unintended consequences instead of immediate relief.

The Real Estate Gold Rush Is Changing Shape

For ordinary investors, the irony is that real estate remains one of the most desired wealth-building assets even while homeownership feels increasingly out of reach.

The broader appeal is easy to understand. Plenty of people still want exposure to real estate returns even if they cannot currently afford a down payment, bidding wars, property taxes, insurance, and a surprise air-conditioning replacement arriving like a financial jump scare in July.

For households trying to navigate those decisions, consulting a financial advisor can help determine whether direct homeownership, fractional real estate investing, or broader diversification makes sense based on income, debt levels, and long-term goals.

Because underneath all the political fighting over Wall Street landlords sits a simpler reality: real estate remains one of the clearest paths to building long-term wealth. The only argument now is who gets access to it first.

data.title: Bill Gates Once Said He Wouldn't Want To Be The Nvidia CEO: 'Jensen Doesn't Even Make His Own Chip...'
data.description:
Gates commended Huang for turning Nvidia into one of the world’s most valuable firms, particularly highlighting Nvidia’s innovative approach, in a February 2025 episode of Opening Bid with Yahoo Finance Executive Editor Brian Sozzi.

The billionaire stressed the importance of design over manufacturing, noting that despite Nvidia not manufacturing their own chips, their design expertise has kept it at the forefront in the competitive tech industry.

‘Doesn’t Even Make His Own Chips’

“And the fact that Jensen doesn’t even make his own chip, you know that everybody has TSMC Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s (NYSE:TSM) available is all the more credit to him,” Gates said.

He said, “at the design level — the silicon, the buses, [and] the interconnects,” Nvidia excels.

However, Gates also acknowledged the fierce competition Huang is up against, saying, "I wouldn't want to be Jensen, necessarily, because you know other people are working on the same things, but you know so far…he has stayed way ahead."

While Nvidia is currently leading the AI chip race, Microsoft and other tech behemoths are also developing their own AI chips while also purchasing from Nvidia, making the future of the industry “pretty hard to predict,” said Bill Gates.

Gates On Huang's Quantum Computing Timeline

However, Gates also acknowledged that Huang may ultimately be right that meaningful progress could take longer due to the technical challenges in both building quantum computers and developing software capable of solving problems beyond classical computers.

As for AI, Bill Gates acknowledged that the industry is experiencing an AI bubble, a view Jensen Huang has repeatedly pushed back against, but argued it is far from a "Tulip Mania" moment and instead more closely resembles the early growth phase of the internet.

In March, Microsoft joined hands with Nvidia to build an AI-powered nuclear energy future, combining Microsoft’s cloud and AI capabilities with Nvidia’s technologies. This collaboration underscores the intertwined future of these tech giants and the importance of AI in shaping the industry.

Photo courtesy: Alexandros Michailidis on Shutterstock

data.title: AMD CEO Lisa Su Says She Was Told As An Engineer To 'Run Towards The Hardest Problems' And That Advice Eventually Led To Her 'Dream Job'
data.description:
Su Learned To Chase Hard Problems

Su, now one of the most prominent leaders in the semiconductor industry, has said that IBM executive John Kelly gave her a lesson that shaped her path.

"I was given a piece of advice when I was a young engineer at IBM that has really stuck with me. Someone told me, ‘You should run toward problems,'" she recalled in an Austin Woman profile. "If you're going to work on something, work on something that's really important."

That mindset later drew her to AMD, a company that had fallen behind its rivals and faced doubts about its future when she joined in 2012. Speaking at Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute's commencement in May 2025, Su said Kelly told her to "run towards the hardest problems," because that is where people find opportunity, learn fastest and grow.

"When I joined, it was clear the company had a mixed track record. But I saw the potential, the people, the vision and the opportunity to help lead a company that mattered … It was actually my dream job," she said.

AMD Turnaround Began With Risk

Su and her family immigrated from Taiwan to the United States when she was a child. The daughter of a bookkeeper and mathematician, she grew up in Queens, New York, took apart and repaired her brother's remote-control cars, and later graduated from the Bronx High School of Science in 1986. She attended the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, where she earned bachelor's, master's and doctoral degrees in electrical engineering.

Huang Also Values Difficult Lessons

Benzinga Edge Stock Rankings indicate AMD stock has a Momentum score in the 98th percentile and a Value score in the 3rd percentile.

Photo Courtesy: jamesonwu1972 on Shutterstock.com

data.title: AI-Driven Shift In Law Enforcement Will Create New Opportunities For Founders, Flock Safety Exec Says
data.description:
Police departments are already preparing for major changes in how officers operate as agencies adopt more AI tools, drones and analytics systems. Flock Safety executive Rahul Sidhu said the shift is creating a major opportunity for startup founders willing to build technology for law enforcement agencies.

Sidhu told founders not to be intimidated by the sector during an appearance on the "A16z" podcast released May 18. "Recognize that that change will come," he said. "It'll come because it's the best thing for them. It's the best thing for the communities. It's the best thing for the country."

Don't Miss:

  • This Under-$1 Pre-IPO AI Company Is Still Open to Retail Investors — Learn More
  • Everyone's watching EV stocks — fewer are paying attention to the lithium powering them

Drones And AI Move Into Public Safety

Technologies once viewed as futuristic could become routine as agencies seek cheaper alternatives to helicopters and traditional surveillance systems, Sidhu said. He pointed to autonomous drones capable of responding to gunshots or tracking suspect vehicles as examples.

"You hear a gunshot go off and the drone finds a shooter getting into a car and driving off and then pursuing the vehicle," he said. "It's kind of almost hard to see that it isn't inevitable."

Flock Safety, a public safety technology platform known for its automated license plate reader systems, is expanding beyond cameras into infrastructure that combines drones, gunshot detection and connected sensor networks.

Sidhu also described how aerial monitoring systems are increasingly being used to assess situations before officers arrive on scene. He recalled one incident where a 911 caller reported a man carrying a shotgun in an alley, but video footage later showed the person was actually a janitor carrying a broom.

Trending: The AI Marketing Platform Backed by Insiders from Google, Meta, and Amazon — Invest at $0.95/Share

Law Enforcement Work Is Changing

Arizona Department of Public Safety Director Col. Jeffrey Glover said on the "A16z" podcast that police departments will need to retrain officers as policing becomes increasingly reliant on AI and automation.

"We know that it's going to be something that's going to really dominate this profession over the next 10 years," he said. "Most of the cops out in the field are going to have to change the way their skill set is shaped."

Departments are increasingly using analytics tied to body camera systems to monitor officer burnout and interactions with the public, Glover said. Those systems can flag potentially combative encounters or identify officers who may need additional support or time away from the field.

He said officers will likely spend more time working with digital evidence, AI systems and fraud detection tools as policing becomes increasingly technical. "It's not going to look the same anymore 10 years from now," Glover added.

Startup Founders Are Being Encouraged To Jump In

Sidhu encouraged founders interested in public safety technology to spend time working directly with police departments rather than building products from a distance.

"Spend time, do some ride-alongs," he said on "A16z". "Really get an understanding of it."

He said founders who spend time in the field will be better positioned to build tools police departments actually adopt.
